top_profane.text:
This is a comparison of the 36” x 78” Pro Impact Mat with Dick’s Sporting Goods 36” X 77” mat. I ordered the Pro Impact, but misunderstood the delivery date. The result was that I also purchased the slightly shorter (only 1 inch) mat from Dick’s so that I would have it when my treadmill was delivered.
Cost: Very similar—Dick’s was $40 and the 4mm Pro Impact was $46
Aesthetic: Dick’s has an attractive finished look. The Pro Impact has an industrial look.
Smell: Dick’s none. Pro Impact has a clear rubber smell. Some reviewers say this goes away as time passes, but still…
Performance: I have only used Dick’s and it absorbs vibration and seems suitable. I am sure Pro Impact would be just as suitable in this regard.
Conclusion: Because of Dick’s superior finish and lack of smell, I would be inclined to buy it again. Side note: Although many treadmills are 80” in length, the portion that actually touches the floor is closer to 75-76 inches. My Sole F63 treadmill fits perfectly on a 77” mat.
top_profane.title: Two Mats Compared

I compared 5 anti-fog coatings on my bathroom mirror: Cat Crap, McNett Sea Drops, Fogtech DX MotoSolutions individually packaged wipes, ordinary Suave shampoo, and Shadow Lake Vanilla Almond castile soap.

I washed the mirror with a Windex-like ammonia cleaner, and rubbed the first 3 on the mirror the day before, using a plastic glove for the cat crap, a microfiber cloth for the McNett's, and the wipe itself for Fogtech. The shampoos I added the next morning. After another 6 hours drying, I buffed them all off with a microfiber cloth (being pretty careful not to use the same section of the cloth on different anti-fog coatings. I left areas between the treatments untreated. Then I took a shower. The mirror was on the wall opposite the shower; I don't think any part of the mirror gets more fogged.

All 5 were vastly superior to no treatment at all. I ranked them, from least to most effective:
5. Ordinary shampoo. The clear loser. The glass wasn't cloudy but had a rippled surface of water sticking to it.
4. Cat Crap. It did pretty well, but had some rippling, and some globs of cat crap that became visible only after steaming.
3. Fogtech. It had stripes in the last direction I'd wiped with the wipe. Although that could have been water dripping from the Castile patch above it. Unfortunately I added the shampoo areas above the other 3, which made
top_profane.title: Comparison of 5 anti-foggers

top_profane.text:
The wobbler did its job and you can easily change the target's direction, elevation, etc. There is one aspect to consider for you all future buyer--keep in mind this aspect is a matter of physics, not the fault of the product itself.

You see, to cock a target launcher you need to use arm strength to pull the launcher arm (or bar, etc) back against spring tension to cock it for the next launch. As you pull on the aluminum launcher arm, the force of the pull has to be opposed by an equal and opposite force. Recall the law as described by Sir Newton. A STATIONARY setup, i.e., without the wobbler kit, it is actually a lot easier to cock the launcher arm. Simply put, you can pull on the launcher arm with one or both hands (and arms) against the stationary clay thrower that's been bolted down tight. As soon as you install the wobbler kit, the law of physic takes over--simply again: this time when you pull on the launcher, your setup spins around as you try to cock the launcher. Makes sense?

I have to use my left hand to pull on the launcher arm while with my right hand grabbing a hold of the body of the clay thrower trying to stabilize it from spinning around. This makes cocking a whole lot more difficult to accomplish. At times I had to try to use my knee to block the launcher from spinning while I use both and to pull and cock the device. Hmmm.
top_profane.title: Wobbler wobbles, naturally . . .

The on/off button shown here is red, but the one on mine is black. The red is a better idea, maybe white (or glow in the dark) would be even better since this is going to be used at night.
Comes with a fender mount, and two sizes of tube mounts. Easy to clip in place and remove from the mounts.
Update Mar 2012: Having used two of these (on my bike and my wifes)for over a year now, I can attest to the usefulness and reliability. No issues, although I have still not been caught in a heavy rain yet with them. We became aware of the availability of different flash patterns when my buddy put two on his commuter bike. My wife also has a Planet Bike Turbo (1 Watt) on her bike. The Radbot seems a bit brighter to me. top_profane.title: Good visible day light - good for group night rides

top_profane.text: This is my second CAS Hanwei/Tinker sword - I also have the non fullered bastard sword - and this is just as well made as the first. Everything is tight and pretty well symmetrical with good grind lines. This blade was sharper than the bastard sword ootb but still needed sharpening. The scabbard is well made but there is significant rattle from the tip. The grip is short, tight and thin so if you have large hands, a handshake grip will be about your only option. The peen isn't bad but it's also not super well done - but what do you expect for a sub $300 sword?  Funnily enough, the Viking feels heavier and less nimble than the bastard even though the bastard is significantly longer - even when using the bastard single handed. I think the Vikings' mass in a shorter but broader length just has more inertia to overcome. Overall, aesthetically I think this is one of the best looking Viking swords out there and I couldn't be happier.
top_profane.title: Nice Viking sword

top_rant.text: I'm pretty amazed by this program. I've tried a ton of exercise dvds, but have never really gotten the eating part down. I've done Weight Watchers,  Nutrisystem, and Diet to Go delivery service; all of which I've loved, but they didn't get me the results I was looking for. I've been pretty athletic most of my life and even when I've fallen off the wagon with exercise it hasn't been a problem reestablishing my fitness level, but the eating part...geez! A few years back, I discovered the Primal way of eating. (Primal is different from Paleo in that it allows full fat dairy if one can tolerate it. I gravitated toward that because I wasn't giving up cream in my coffee. Ever.) Anyhow, eating Primally helped me lose weight. Plus, I felt better. Not so fatigued. Whenever I'd go back to eating grains my heart would race and I just felt weird. The sugar part I had down pat. I'd discovered stevia before I discovered Primal style eating so I didn't worry at all about sugar content in my diet if I were doing the cooking. Although, for me, Primal style eating was healthier (again, for me!) I'd veer off the trail and go back to eating a SAD (Standard American Diet) diet just because I'd get lazy or work was overwhelming or I just wanted something quick and easy (fast food). So I remained on a yo yo with my weight. I lost and gained the same freaking 10-15 lbs constantly! It was so annoying.

I just got a Crosman Fury: Even though it's a piston and not a springer it still is hold sensitive. Making sure to put my fingers on the stock exactly the same each shot I'm better with the Fury the first day than with my Gamo Silent Cat after 3 months!

I still love my Silent Cat, but the Fury is: heavy metal and well built; 4x scope not 3x; no iron sights but no iron sights to interfere with the view through the scope; the barrel is smaller so most pellets have to be pushed in not just dropped in loosely; tap to break barrel open and a bit harder to cock; nicer trigger; the noise is an old fashioned air pop instead of a spring twang so it's louder than the Cat but way, way easier to bullseye over and over with any pellet I choose, even cheap Destroyers! All for half the price of the Gamo! I love both, but the Gamo is for silent 4am de-raccooning of my roof, while the Fury is for bullseyes on paper indoors.

Benjamin Hunting Hollow Points (750 tin of domed hollow point)
Beeman Silver Arrow (pointed, huge 11.5g)
Crosman Premier Hollow Points
Gamo Bone Collector (domed hollow point)
Crosman Premier Super Match (flat top wadcutter)
Diablo Basic (green tin of flat top wadcutter)
Crosman Wadcutter Premium Grade (tin of 250, premium not premier)

top_rant.text: As a retired US Army Ranger, I can attest to the many uses that parachute cord has. It's lightweight, thin, super strong, and doesn't absorb water like non synthetic cordage. "Paracord" has pretty much caught on in almost every culture because of its versatility, availability in surplus stores and sporting goods stores, and of course "reality TV" (which is an oxymoron, those shows are about the furthest thing from reality there is lol) shows about "preppers". I actually find it really humorous that people walk around with bracelets meant to be "fashion statements" which are made of the same stuff we used to use to tie crap up with in the field in the Army!  What most people aren't aware of is that there are multiple grades of parachute cord used by the US Armed Forces. There are 4 types I-IV, with I being the lightest, and IV being the strongest. The majority of the parachute cord available is either genuine military "Type III", which is rated to be able to hold 550lbs without breaking, which is why it's sometimes referred to as "550 cord", or commercial cord which looks pretty much the same, is about the same thickness, and generally is "about" (some is weak and crappy, some is actually stronger) as strong. Hi, I got these PINEMEADOW GOLF CLUBS & THE PUTTER for my Grandson Dylan and he loved these clubs, so much that he would go out golfing just about every chance he got, after work, on the weekends! Dylan was really loving his new toys, it was an outlet for him... you see Dylan works a pretty stressful job climbing towers 100`s of feet in the air and so we all can have phone and internet service.
BUT then something happened that changed all of that. On November 5, 2016 Dylan and my Granddaughter Savannah & their 2 babies, my great grand babies, Kameron & Nathan were in a gas explosion. It blew up everything, their apartment, their vehicles, all their furniture, and personal belongings, included the GOLF CLUBS & NEW PUTTER.... Now I'm not one for material items being that important and I was just glad they were all alive...BUT...these clubs meant a lot to Dylan and I had thought I had it covered, I have the Manufacturer Warranty and also I had bought a 2 yr extended Warranty as well. NO WORRIES I THOUGHT... I told Dylan I would call the companies and see which one will replace the clubs and not to worry he would be able to golf again and relieve which now is not just stress but the doctors told them they had PTSD from the explosion.
DO YOU KNOW NOT ONE FRICKIN` COMPANY WOULD REPLACE THESE CLUBS & PUTTER, NOT ONE!
EVERYONE I CALLED, STARTING WITH WHERE I ORIGINAL
top_screaming.title: Great golf clubs and putters... BUT they don't honor their WARRANTIES or their CUSTOMERS! please read my story!

I was looking for a somewhat light but not crazy expensive tent to use while backpacking... when I was unable / didn't want to use my hammock. I took a risk on this tent - after using it a few times, this is what I've learned.
(This is the current model as of mid-fall 2015 - Lime green body, light grey fly, light green poles.)

The size is perfect for me. 5' 10", 160lbs. Someone a little longer / heavier would be ok, but not Much Bigger. The vestibule is perfect for my trail runners and my mostly empty 45L pack (IE, no sleeping bag, no sleeping pad, no tent... there really is very little left in the pack). People complain about the vestibule... and if Erueka made it bigger, the fly would be heavier. This is a minimalist lightweight tent. The user needs to be of that mindset too. Just IMHO.

The weight is good. I ditched everything but the following...
Body: 19 oz
Poles: 8.8 oz
Fly: 16.3 oz

I seam sealed (aerosol spray) the hell out of the fly and the lower seams on the body. 3 coats.

I added one thing, a piece of Tyvek... cut just a bit smaller than the body. Washed 3.2 oz.

Here's a tip: You don't need stuff sacks for everything. Line your pack with a good quality garbage bag and you're set. Wrap the tent + fly in the Tyvek + elastic band, then into the pack. Poles on the outside of the p
top_five_star_obscene.title: Light = check.  Cheap = check.  Good = check.

Did a lot of research on static flushes to remove salt deposits and ended up going with this gallon of Rydlyme and I am glad I did!

Let me tell you what my process was so you can save time and get the best results. You will need a small trash can or a receptacle large enough to hold a couple gallons of water and a pump. For the pump I tried a 1,200 gal per hour bilge pump but it was not strong enough to force water vertical up through the motor. I ended up with a basic 1/3 HP submersible pump from Home depot and it was perfect it forced that Rydlyme through that motor like a dream!

Now where did I flow the water? I removed the thermostat housing from the top of the engine, then removed the thermostat (thermostat was stuck in the open position due to salt formation so its been replaced not) then I got a heater hose from home depot that fit over the end of the thermostat housing and to the submersible pump. Then reinstall the thermostat cover with no thermostat in. Next i pulled all the anode plates off the motor, removed the anodes (replaced those after flush) and put plates back on.

Now with the pump submerged in the rydlme and water inside the trash can I positioned it under the motor (lower unit removed to protect impellar). Once I plugged in the pump that rydlyme mixture shot up through the hose and down through the top of my motor via
top_five_star_obscene.title: RYDLYME cured my engine!  PLEASE READ!

The first one I got was a generic one from some random grocery store. It was small and barely kept my drinks cold for an hour. It also annoyed me because I always had to completely unscrew the cap to start drinking what was inside. I wanted one that had an easy way to open it, especially if I was walking and carrying books and only had one hand.

So after a while, when I was at the store I found a different water bottle that had a lid that flipped open with the press of a button. This was nice as it made it easy to get that delicious beverage in me, but it was also small and didn't hold its temperate very well. It also had a very small opening so it was hard to clean.

This is a gladiator 1. I was debating if I should get the G2 or G3. -The darts were fine! People keep saying how the supplied darts are generally crap but maybe I don't know better. I did order another set from ebay however so we will see
-The board itself looks brsnd new and the sisal seems very nice. Very few bounceouts and the holes seems to close up well

We decided to put it on a door off the LR and when not in use we can close it and have it out of the way. I used an over the door hook and screwed the supplied mounting hardware into it so there are NO holes drilled into the door. I also used some velcro dots to maintain stability and you can see I used a 2 by 2 by 1 inch DIY wall/door foam protector. Working great! Excellent seller and excellent product!

These are not high quality glasses. If that is what you are looking for stop reading this, prepare to spend 5 to 10 ten times as much as these cost, and click away. If you are like me and will likely lose or break every pair of sunglasses in the first 6 months of owning them, and are tired of dropping hundreds of dollars to replace them, then read on.

I bought these as a backup pair of mountain biking glasses. I specifically was looking for a pair that had several low light lenses as I have lost all my previous ones.

When I opened the package my first thought was, "These feel like cheap crap". I was right, they are cheap crap, but that is what you get for just over $20. Still, I put the clear lens in and went for a night ride on one of my local trails. The super cheap plastic is near weightless and the 2 cent nose piece is surprisingly comfortable. They are a little large for someone with a smaller head but the rubber ear pieces slid up under my helmet in a very comfortable way that made them extremely stable. After a few minutes I had almost forgot I was wearing them except when a large bug would collide with the lens and remind me they were working.

This review will focus on the specs since there is minimal information available unless you buy the bike.

First off, this is my second Mongoose. I started out on a crappy low end Cruiser model, so anything even half a step up from that model is a vast improvement.

The Theory weighs about 38 lbs and is actually light grey in color despite the image on this website. I thought I was getting a white bike, and I'm a little ticked it's actually grey, but, whateva.

Assembly isn't too bad as long as you have half a clue as to what you're doing. The documentation is adequate, but don't expect to be able to assemble this bike correctly if you haven't done it before. The documentation will frustrate you. But the average person can probably install the pedals and the saddle, and maybe even mount the handlbars correctly assuming the brakes don't arrive screwed up like mine did. The front cable had to be relocated because when it was assembled at the plant, it was threaded immediately under the fork and the tire was rubbing against it so it had to be removed and resituated, which entailed messing with the front cable. Simple enough, I suppose, but this assumes you know what you're doing.

top_five_star_obscene.text:
I've purchased these ammo cans 2 at a time for a total of 4. These are new manufactured highest quality ammo cans. Can you save a few bucks buying some used military surplus? Of course you could. What you're buying is (in my case) is a 100% protection for expensive ammunition (expensive to me anyways). Is anyone truly able to buy inexpensive ammo these days? Buy it because it's new, buy it because the seal is perfect and new, buy it because it truly protects what ammo you put in them for years, if you desire. Buy it because it's not a cheap piece of crap like some others. Buy it because it hasn't been thrown around all to hell and back and lost its ability to seal. Why do you think new military ammunition in virtually every country in the world
puts their new ammunition in new cans from the beginning? Because it works... And not to over dramatize it, but lives sometimes depend on it. I hope this ever isn't the case for you but, saving that 10 bucks on a crappy used 30 year old rusty and worn out can.... May just do that some day. My own rifle ammo costs too much for me to put it something that may not be the best choice. You get what you pay for when it comes to ammo storage and protection from water and other ammo killing elements.

I asked my son what he wanted for his 8th grade graduation. He says, “oh I don’t know. Maybe just a few tennis balls”. I was thinking I’d be buying some Xbox or PlayStation crap so if my kid decides he wants “a few tennis balls”, I’m going nuts!

So naturally I ordered him a crap ton of these. Now I’m not really a pro tennis player. Actually I’ve only played real tennis twice in high school and then decided that was enough so I don’t know how to accurately judge a quality tennis ball. But my kid was excited as hell. They bounce. Dogs have chewed them and they didn’t tear apart right away and they launch off a baseball bat. So I’m going with these being some A+ tennis balls.
